content:
# Chapter 7: Setting Up Your Dev Environment

*In which we install some software, open a terminal, and agree not to panic.*

## The Setup Chapter Nobody Wants to Read

Every developer tutorial has a setup chapter. Nobody likes it. Everyone wants to skip straight to building.

Please don't skip this one.

A poorly configured environment causes about 40% of beginner frustration. "Why is it not working?" is usually not a logic error — it's a missing dependency, a wrong Node version, or an environment variable that didn't load. This chapter prevents those problems.

We'll keep it focused: only what you need for building AI apps. No optional extras.

---

## What We're Installing

- **Node.js** — JavaScript runtime (runs JavaScript outside of a browser)
- **npm / pnpm** — Package managers (install libraries)
- **Git** — Version control (track changes, use GitHub)
- **VS Code** — Code editor
- **A terminal** — The command-line interface
- **A `.env` setup** — How to manage secrets safely

That's it. No databases yet. No cloud accounts yet. Just the local foundation.

---

## The Terminal: Stop Fearing It

The terminal (also called the command line, shell, or console) is a text interface to your computer. You type commands; the computer executes them.

**On Mac:** It's called Terminal. Open it with Spotlight (Cmd + Space, type "terminal").

**On Windows:** Use Windows Terminal with PowerShell, or install WSL (Windows Subsystem for Linux) for a proper Unix environment. WSL is recommended for development.

**On Linux:** You already know where your terminal is.

Basic commands you'll use constantly:

```bash
pwd           # Print working directory (where am I?)
ls            # List files in current directory
cd folder     # Change directory into "folder"
cd ..         # Go up one directory
mkdir myapp   # Make a new directory called "myapp"
touch file.js # Create an empty file
cat file.js   # Print file contents to terminal
rm file.js    # Delete a file (careful — no undo)
```

> 📌 **Remember This**
> The terminal feels slow at first. It gets fast with practice. Most experienced developers prefer terminal for development tasks — it's precise, scriptable, and doesn't require hunting through menus.

---

## Installing Node.js

Node.js lets you run JavaScript on your computer (not just in a browser). It comes with npm (Node Package Manager) for installing libraries.

**Recommended approach: Use nvm (Node Version Manager)**

nvm lets you install and switch between Node versions — important because different projects sometimes need different Node versions.

**Mac/Linux:**
```bash
# Install nvm
curl -o- https://raw.githubusercontent.com/nvm-sh/nvm/v0.39.0/install.sh | bash

# Restart your terminal, then install Node
nvm install 20
nvm use 20
nvm alias default 20
```

**Windows:** Use nvm-windows (github.com/coreybutler/nvm-windows)

**Verify it worked:**
```bash
node --version   # Should print v20.x.x
npm --version    # Should print 10.x.x
```

> 🐇 **Bunny Tip**
> Always install the LTS (Long Term Support) version of Node for new projects. It's the most stable. The current LTS at time of writing is Node 20.

---

## Setting Up a Project

Create a new project:

```bash
mkdir my-ai-app
cd my-ai-app
npm init -y          # Creates package.json with defaults
```

Install the OpenAI SDK:

```bash
npm install openai
```

Install a few other things you'll need throughout the book:

```bash
npm install dotenv   # Loads environment variables from .env
npm install express  # Web server framework (for the API backend)
```

Your project folder now has:
```
my-ai-app/
├── node_modules/     # Installed packages (never commit this to git)
├── package.json      # Project metadata and dependency list
└── package-lock.json # Exact version lock for reproducible installs
```

---

## Environment Variables: The Right Way to Handle Secrets

An environment variable is a value stored in your operating system's environment, not in your code. This is how you keep API keys out of source code.

**Step 1:** Create a `.env` file in your project root:

```
OPENAI_API_KEY=sk-your-actual-api-key-here
```

**Step 2:** Create a `.gitignore` file to make sure `.env` never gets committed to git:

```
node_modules/
.env
.env.local
.env.production
```

**Step 3:** Load the environment variables in your code:

```javascript
import 'dotenv/config';  // This loads .env automatically

// Now you can access your key:
console.log(process.env.OPENAI_API_KEY);  // Your key, safely loaded
```

> ⚠️ **Watch Out!**
> Check your `.gitignore` is working before you `git add` anything. Run `git status` and make sure `.env` isn't listed. If it is, your `.gitignore` isn't set up right. Fix it before committing.

---

## Installing VS Code

VS Code (Visual Studio Code) is the most popular code editor for web development. It's free, fast, and has an enormous ecosystem of extensions.

Download from code.visualstudio.com.

**Extensions to install immediately:**

- **Prettier** — Auto-formats your code on save. Eliminates all arguments about indentation.
- **ESLint** — Catches JavaScript errors before you run the code.
- **GitLens** — Supercharges the built-in git integration.
- **GitHub Copilot** — AI pair programmer. Autocompletes code as you type. (Requires a paid subscription but has a free tier for students.)

**VS Code settings to turn on:**
- Format on Save: Open settings (Cmd/Ctrl + ,), search "format on save", check the box.
- Auto save: Search "auto save", set to "afterDelay".

---

## Setting Up Git

Git is version control — it tracks every change to your code, lets you undo mistakes, and enables collaboration.

**Install:**
- Mac: `brew install git` (if you have Homebrew) or download from git-scm.com
- Windows: Download from git-scm.com
- Linux: `sudo apt install git`

**Initial configuration:**
```bash
git config --global user.name "Your Name"
git config --global user.email "you@example.com"
```

**Initialize a git repo for your project:**
```bash
cd my-ai-app
git init
git add .
git commit -m "Initial project setup"
```

**The three commands you'll use 90% of the time:**
```bash
git status          # What changed?
git add .           # Stage all changes
git commit -m "msg" # Save a snapshot with a message
```

> 🐇 **Bunny Tip**
> Commit often. Small, frequent commits ("add chat endpoint", "fix error handling") are far better than one massive commit ("everything"). You can always revert to any commit. Think of commits as save points in a video game.

---

## GitHub: Where Your Code Lives

GitHub (github.com) is a website that hosts git repositories. It's where the world's open source code lives and where you'll back up your projects.

Create an account. Create a new repository. Follow the instructions to push your local project to GitHub:

```bash
git remote add origin https://github.com/yourusername/my-ai-app.git
git push -u origin main
```

Your code is now safely backed up and accessible from anywhere.

---

## Testing Your Setup

Let's make sure everything works. Create a file called `test.js`:

```javascript
import 'dotenv/config';
import OpenAI from 'openai';

const client = new OpenAI();

const response = await client.chat.completions.create({
  model: 'gpt-3.5-turbo',
  messages: [{ role: 'user', content: 'Say "setup successful" and nothing else.' }],
});

console.log(response.choices[0].message.content);
```

Update `package.json` to enable ES modules:
```json
{
  "type": "module"
}
```

Run it:
```bash
node test.js
```

If you see "Setup successful" (or something close), everything is working. If you see an error, the most common causes are:

- API key not in `.env` file, or `.env` file named wrong
- `dotenv` not installed (`npm install dotenv`)
- Wrong path to your project

> 🔨 **Try It Yourself**
> Do the full setup above. Install Node, create a project, add your API key to `.env`, run `test.js`. Don't move to Chapter 8 until this works. Everything in Part 3 builds on this foundation.

---

## Summary

- Terminal: basic navigation commands — `cd`, `ls`, `pwd`, `mkdir`
- Node.js: install via nvm, verify with `node --version`
- Environment variables: keep secrets in `.env`, add `.env` to `.gitignore`
- VS Code: download, install Prettier, ESLint, Copilot
- Git: `git status`, `git add .`, `git commit -m`
- Test your setup before moving on
